Still some crashes

## Case 1

Variants list crashes when cancer tier key is int

To reproduce:
1. Add entry as custom term `{ "key": 6, "label": "Tier achtung", "description": "Wir haben nur ein stuhl", "label_class": "default" }`
2. Goto a cancer case
3. Classify as tier achtung
4. Goto variants list view

## Case 2

Variants list view crashes if an old evaluation term has been removed

To reproduce
1. Add a custom dismissal term to the database, `{ "key": 60, "label": "Our major issue", "description": "Major major issue", "evidence": ["pileup", "GT"] }`
2. Goto a variant for a case
3. Dismiss variant with the term "Our major issue"
4. Remove the dismissal term from the database
5. Go to <case name>/variants

The python 3.8 CI tests are failing because https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/14519 was introduced in astropy 5.3.  Photutils supports astropy >= 5.0 and the 3.8 CI tests use astropy 5.2.2.  You'll need to update the tests comparing the output flux uncertainties only for astropy 5.3+ (e.g., see https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.utils.introspection.minversion.html).

I think it might be worth doing another iteration on the naming before we publicize, but that doesn't need to block this.  We should mark these experimental though.

The `current_time` argument that we provide to `get_partition_keys` is a Pendulum datetime, not a float. Thoughts on doing that here? An advantage is that, for someone writing their own FreshnessPolicy, they can look at the hour/minute/whatever without needing to convert it.  Same with the `upstream_materialization_timestamps`.

A couple freshness policies I brainstormed:
* The asset is in violation of policy if it hasn't been updated in the last 20 minutes (i.e. ancestors are not considered)
* The asset is in violation of policy if it hasn't been updated within 20 minutes of the most recent materialization of any of its parents.

Would those be easy to implement? Not requesting that you implement them in this PR, just want to make sure the API works for them.

High-level structure comment is it's a bit long at the moment, and we go through quite a bit of content before reaching the actual part where we deploy to a cluster. Some suggestions to make it more digestible:
- Consider moving Kubernetes out into its own guide and make it clear that the quickstart is for local dev only
- Consider ways to reduce the content before the first deployment. Maybe think about taking any optional configs and moving those out to their own page on "Configuring a Serve deployment in production" or something like that.

Hello @hemanth-silabs 

Thank you for this contribution, it's an important step towards more secure products! Good work!

I have an overall question about architecture. Right now the code is sliced with ifdefs in many places to choose between PSA API and mbedTLS API.

Haven't you considered using only the PSA API (whenever possible)? As far as I understand, we can update the mbedTLS version to one that supports PSA API (e.g. 2.25 from TF-M 1.3) - I have it done on a branch and can contribute. Since for non-TF-M type of devices, PSA API implementation translates code to mbedTLS API under the hood anyway. For example the `psa_hash_update` will call `mbedtls_sha256_update_ret` - [code](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/v2.25.0/library/psa_crypto.c#L2655). The only caveat is that for such non-TF-M devices, we would need to implement ITS API for the key-by-reference usage.

I have two small notes for when you make future open source contributions.

1. I recommend creating a new branch from your fork, instead of directly committing to `master` there. That makes it possible to work on multiple pull requests at once, and ensures that there will never be commits on `master` of your fork that don't exist on the upstream project.

To me, this is still too much of a speed hit for something that will be called very regularly and is purely for maintainability. The errors were quite rare—they only happened three or four times since the first report on July 6 (#320), which means hundreds of CI runs. Perhaps they could be induced on a Mac by re-running that test in a tight loop, but then again, maybe that sets up the memory in such a way that new allocations hit a lucky spot of memory 100% of the time.

I created a branch (not a PR!) to try to make the bug more likely:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/compare/master...jpivarski/intermittent_error_bugfix-DEBUG

It is relative to master, so the bug should still be there. (I didn't want to take the chance that your PR fixed it, because then we'd be chasing nothing!) This assigns `-999` to all elements of all Indexes and integers that are to be filled before filling them in `argsort_next` with the idea that some value might be left unfilled and we're looking at uninitialized memory. That's probably not it because doing so didn't cause an error on Linux, and a value of `-999` ought to cause some problems. (I also tried switching them to positive `999`, in case it's something that's just compared with zero, like in IndexedOptionArray. That didn't trigger it either, on Linux.)

If you check out this branch and run it on MacOS, does it make the bug show itself? (What about changing all the `-999` to `999`? Adding more or bigger `little_arrays` in `test_IndexedOffsetArray`? In Python 2 vs 3?) If none of that brings out the bug, then perhaps we should just apply your possible fix and keep an eye on it for the next few months.

---------------------------------------

There are a lot of changes here unrelated to the bug; they're C++ features from clang-tidy. Some of them explicitly define or delete special functions (copy/assignment constructors, etc.), some of them add `explicit` and `final` keywords, which I'm not familiar with, though I've just read up on them. (I'm not sure why we're setting `final` on Forms; the class hierarchies are very flat, most concrete classes directly inherit from a single abstract class. Unintended subclassing is not something we're likely to do.) From the documentation, these seem to be exclusively C++11 features, which is required, though I don't know if CI would catch a C++14 feature, if one were accidentally used.

I've written inline comments about initializing class members to `0`. I don't know if that's related to the clang-tidy or if that was intended to fix the bug. Either way, I'd rather not do that because it gives the impression that constructors don't have to assign them explicitly. Everything here should be 100% RAII, with all class members set by every constructor. If there's a way to enforce it with a C++11 feature, I'd be up for that.

An annoying question: is this something that should be in pybind11, rather than say ... an external plugin `pybind11_filesystem`? I was first skeptical about this, but the actual implementation is rather clean/small, so I guess I'm OK with this.

This would allow for other C++ path-like type to quickly opt-in to this behavior, if they support a minimal interface (be constructible/assignable from `std::string` and having `.native()` or so).

I came across another general issue. We currently filter out all unplayable tracks and this means we'll be removing them whenever we make any playlist change, which is wrong. Tracks might be currently unplayable if unavailable in the geo region where the user is logged in (but may be playable from elsewhere and/or in the future). I think local files might also be treated as unplayable but I'm not sure if Spotify still supports this feature I never used it. I think to workaround this we'll need to change the current playlist behaviour (which has been the same since our first implementation) to now include unplayable tracks in playlists. libspotify won't be able to load these tracks when it comes to try and play them and they will be skipped with an `info` log message (which we will want to adjust). These unplayable tracks will clutter the queue/playlist listing and might be confusing to users when they try to play them. But I don't see another way to avoid losing them when we use `save()`.

I've not looked closely at the myers implementation here, I assume it's the normal one that's available everywhere. It seems odd that in 2020 there is no standard, fast (C), robust implementation available for us to just pull in and use. It's not ideal we have to reproduce (and test) our own version of this common algorithm. But we do have Python's standard `difflib.SequenceMatcher`, so what does using myers provide over just using that? I did find multiple (why???) packages for Google's [diff-match-patch(https://github.com/google/diff-match-patch) library. That seems to use many of the optimisations we originally talked about in #236 but I've no great interest in using that, seems overly complicated. 

At a higher level, some of what has been added to playlist.py belongs in web.py. You'll notice there isn't currently any Spotify Web API specific stuff in playlist.py. I think we want to keep that separation.

Wow, @DaelonSuzuka , that's indeed a lot of work—thanks! Its a sufficiently big extension of QtPy (with many new modules, new errors, and other changes) that I think it makes sense to ship it in 1.2, assuming @dalthviz agrees.

I highly recommend applying my GitHub suggestions first, which are generally straightforward and uncontroversial, before addressing the higher level comments and feedback, assuming you don't disagree with them; otherwise, they are likely to get outdated/lost.

Some high-level feedback and suggestions:

* [ ] Right now, since QtPy is still using the implicit ("flat") package layout rather than the now-recommended explicit `src` layout, this means that `module_report` is a top-level import package equal to `qtpy` itself and will be included in the distribution package and installed in the user's environment as a separate import package, which is of course not what we want. We could exclude it with some futzing in the setuptools configuration, but IMO its still confusing to have two top-level import packages, one of which is just a development-focused helper tool. 

  Therefore, after consultation with you and @dalthviz , I suggest we do one of the following:
  
  * Stick `module_report` inside a `tools` directory (which wouldn't be importable and is ignored by default in the new Setuptools 61 auto-discovery) and run it from there when needed *(simplest solution to the immediate problem)*
  * If we think this functionality is likely to be useful to other developers using QtPy to check their environments, we _could_ add it as a subpackage of the actual `qtpy` package, and add it as a command to the QtPy development CLI. *(Somewhat nicer usability for us and allows other devs to use it to, if that has value, though a little more effort and means it is part of QtPy itself)*
  * Make `module_report` its own separate repo/distribution package (Better usability for us and others without bundling it in QtPy itself, and if you want to help maintain it, you could much more easily be added as a Collaborator on that repo, but is more initial work, more overhead and less directly coupled to QtPy's own development)

  Making it a separate repo, installable (at least) from GitHub, is the "nicest" solution, but also the most work, up front and (to some extent) over time, so whether this is viable depends on whether you want to help maintain it, and also on if it more generally useful to application developers rather than just development of QtPy itself. Making it a subpackage of `qtpy` buys us the latter benefit, if it has significant value, with less work, but also means that it gets shipped to all users, few of which are likely to need it.
  
  So IMO, just keeping it in a `tools` directory is probably the best approach for now unless and until those other conditions are a compelling enough reason to do otherwise (which we could always do in the future). 

* [ ] If we're going to check the output files into the repo, we should run `module_report` in our CIs to catch any changes and either update the files or take other action accordingly. However, given the results can vary between OSes (e.g. `DBus` on Windows) and distributions (`pip`/`conda`), it might be better to just not check them in directly, or if so make the checked-in files specifically for comparison to a per-platform CI check that determines if the output matches the expected (but that latter part would be *optional* for this PR). This could also help automate and decouple the testing from the details of the implementation, which you brought up some valid concerns about—but again, that could go in a future PR.

* [ ] Instead of making all the exception messages base `PythonQtError`s (such that there's no way for tools to programmatically determine whether the module was not installed, not supported on that binding, or some other error occurred) and writing manual messages for each one (which is way more work to add, check and change, and easier to make a typo on), we should subclass it with specific errors of each type, that take a `name` and a `binding` argument (with a `msg` override) and construct the message from there. This also allows us to have it inherit the built-in `ModuleNotFoundError` so it behaves as expected.
  
    Therefore, I suggest adding the following to the `__init__.py` under the existing errors and using them accordingly:

    ```python
     class QtModuleNotFoundError(ModuleNotFoundError, PythonQtError):
        """Raised when a Python Qt binding submodule is not installed/supported."""
        _msg = 'The {name} module was not found for {binding}.'
        _msg_extra = ''

        def __init__(self, *, name, binding=None, msg=None, **msg_kwargs):
            self.binding = binding
            msg = msg or f'{self._msg} {self._msg_extra}'.strip()
            msg = msg.format(name=name, binding=binding, **msg_kwargs)
            super().__init__(msg, name=name)

    class QtBindingMissingModuleError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raised when a module is not supported by a given binding."""
        _msg_extra = 'It is not currently implemented in {binding}.'

    class QtModuleNotInstalledError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raise when a module is supported by the binding, but not installed."""
        _msg_extra = 'It must be installed separately'

        def __init__(self, *, missing_package=None, **superclass_kwargs):
            self.missing_package = missing_package
            if missing_package is not None:
                 self._msg_extra += ' as {missing_package}.'
            super().__init__(missing_package=missing_package, **superclass_kwargs)
    ```

Also, a few smaller but common issues (which I made suggestions to fix, except where noted):

* [ ] Let's please not use `*` imports in `module_report`, as they are considered a very bad practice and make the code hard to understand, introspect and lint, among other reasons. It is true that QtPy uses them internally, but for a very specific purpose—ensuring it picks up the full namespace of the packages it wraps without hardcoding—that isn't the case here. Also, we should use standard, conventional PEP 8 import ordering.
* [ ] Module-level constants are ALL_CAPS per PEP 8 and standard convention
* [ ] Don't forget to add terminating EOLs to the files
* [ ] Per PEP 257, docstrings must end with periods and one-line docstrings should be on one line (as they are currently), not with `'''"` above and below (I didn't make review comments for this to avoid noise)

Thanks, and looking forward to seeing this in final form!

Regarding removal of `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`/`AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, not sure if this is a great user experience - so far, the premise was always that `awslocal` should ideally work out of the box, using sensible defaults where needed. ```

make_bucket failed: s3://foo Unable to locate credentials
```

Could we maybe attempt to create a boto3 client, which will go through the credentials resolver chain, and then selectively only set individual environment variable defaults if the client cannot be created? Something like the following (not fully fleshed out, just conceptually):
```
try:
    boto3.client("sts").get_caller_identity()
except botocore.exceptions.NoCredentialsError:
    env_dict['AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID'] = 'test'
    env_dict['AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY'] = 'test'
    # optionally, we could try again, to ensure it works
    # boto3.client("sts").get_caller_identity()
```

This will add a tiny fraction to the runtime of `awslocal` commands, but I think it would make for a better user experience. Also, this way we could still make use of profiles and `~/.aws/config`.

I see that you've put a lot of work into this and appreciate it, and to that end I've tried to give a more thorough review of the potential issues I see in this implementation.

However, I do have some structural concerns that give me pause about merging this:
* There are still various cases that may or may not be handled properly in all scenarios, in particular, at the very least I some issues I see with unions, custom subclasses of `list`, and custom subclasses of `AbstractSet`.
* This essentially aims to duplicate one conceptual part of the logic of model validation, but doesn't follow the same code paths or anything, and is likely to not exactly match the existing behavior in various cases (including the ones called out in the previous point). And without duplicating a huge swath of tests, it seems to me highly likely that we will eventually introduce a change in the validation logic (e.g., more config settings) that won't be properly respected by the recursive construct function.
* I'll also note that adding these `if` branches into the `model_construct` method will have performance implications (even if small) for non-users of the recursion.

Given all the potential issues here, I suspect that a _better_ way to implement this would be to create a `construct` function in pydantic-core which differs from validation in precisely the ways you want — basically, handling everything besides a `BaseModel` subclass as `Any`, with special treatment for unions/generic containers that contain `BaseModel` subclasses at some level down in their parameters.

I think that would be much more performant, and also much easier to keep in sync with the behavior of the non-model-construct logic in the library, and if implemented well, easier to describe the behavior.

If we continue down the path of this implementation, I suspect there will be a lot of subtle inconsistencies/misbehaviors with this feature, and it will be a source of issue reports we need to maintain for some time; as a result, I think the other maintainers are likely to be averse to accepting a feature like this, but I'll let them speak for themselves — @samuelcolvin , @adriangb, anyone else who sees this..

If the other maintainers agree with me that this seems like it would be a _significant_ maintenance burden to add, I think an approach that might be more likely to get this integrated into the library in the long term would be to create a separate package on PyPI called something like `pydantic-recursive-construct` that implements recursive model construct as a standalone function, and get some adoption and work out any kinks. Then re-open a PR on pydantic once the implementation is a bit more battle tested. But again, I'm not sure what the other maintainers might have to say.

Some things I noticed:

In the simple.py example we now fuzz all non-combinatorial cases for message1, then all for message1->message2, then we continue with the combinatorial cases for message1, and finally those for message1->message2.
Wouldn't be more straight forward to first fuzz all cases of a request before switching to the next one? Or is that the point you mentioned that's just too much work?

Also the progress bar in the webinterface seems to be broken, showing either Infinity or NaN:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46794237/111071994-f1d4f580-84d8-11eb-80b1-56f7e10e7f0f.png)

A simple request like...
```python
s_initialize("Test")
if s_block_start("test1"):
    s_delim("1")
    s_delim("2")
s_block_end("Test")
```
...now yields way more cases than expected.
From the log: `Info: Type: Delim. Default value: b''. Case 3960 of 88 overall.`

That leads to my next question, is the combinatorial fuzzing enabled for all primitives now? Because if so we would now generate a lot more cases than before. Even with just two delims from the example above we yield about 50 times more cases.
I see this problematic for large request definitions which would suddenly generate millions of cases which one simply cannot send to a target in an acceptable time.
Would it be possible to add a switch to session or perhaps fuzzable to enable/disable combinatorial fuzzing?

That being said, there is an existing way to admit casting from `py::none` to a different representation (custom object) by using `py::detail::type_caster<>`.
Docs: https://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/cast/custom.html
Example (in pybind11) for `std::optional<T>` caster:
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/484b0f04332fff15543d4c2a49c355d6942455fa/include/pybind11/stl.h#L281-L283

Therefore, could you better qualify your commit / PR title to reflect that?
e.g.
"fix: allow casting from None to custom object when using type_caster_generic" (impl details)
or
"fix: allow casting from None to custom object for generic types"

High level comment for the group - do we want to support the following - 
- [Fast and Accurate Model Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.06877) proposes a way to scale models. Once a model is scaled, it cannot be defined by `w_a`, `w_m`, etc. anymore and instead needs to be specified by the stage widths, group widths, etc. which means the `RegNetParams` API does not suffice. We already have models like these being trained currently with multiple downstream applications (happy to share details internally). In Classy Vision we had an `AnyNetParams` API for this (https://github.com/facebookresearch/ClassyVision/blob/b9ea86b39cc469867aabb76ada33bf5a8530eb19/classy_vision/models/anynet.py#L364), which `RegNetParams` derived from.
- The [Designing Network Design Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.13678) paper specified AnyNets as fairly general networks and `RegNetX`, `RegNetY`, `RegNetZ` are instantiations of these. Which is why we have multiple block types - a user can specify very general networks with these settings. 

IMO at the very least we should support instantiating scaled models. Supporting general AnyNets might be out of scope for torchvision. Answering these two questions is important to answer some of the comments in code currently!

A bit more involved case analysis but looking correct to me.

Logging some very helpful out-of-band comments from Ian here:

```
We look at the external schema (the one that generates the package for Foo) and run the generator pre-processor which determines what types are generated.
```

This is good. So we are improving on not generating dangling references. 

However backwards-compatibiltiy remains a bit fragile, there are unexpected constraints. To cite Ian's example:

```
Adding a new resource that references an old type in an optional way is breaking if if the type was not previously referenced in an optional way.  That is confusing, so here is a concrete example:
Given a schema:
resources:
  fooConfigurator:
    inputProperties:
      input:
        $ref: #/types/foo
        required: true
types:
  foo:
    prop1:
      type: string
    prop2:
      type: string
Adding the new resource:
resources:
  takesOptionalFoo:
    input:
      $ref: #/types/foo
is a breaking change to downstream packages.
If there is a downstream package fooConsumer with schema:
resources:
  fooConsumer:
    inputProperties:
      input:
        $ref: foo#/types/foo
Before the schema of foo is updated, fooConsumer will generate the following go
type FooConsumerArgs struct {
  Input *foo.FooInput
}
but after the change (and consuming the new schema) fooConsumer will generate
type FooConsumerArgs struct {
  Input foo.FooPtrInput
}
This changes the public interface of fooConsumer in a breaking way.
```

We do not have a solution to the fundamental problem here, namely that we do global schema analysis to try to avoid generating unused helper types, but we miss that downstream packages may decide to use them; and therefore global changes to the schema may result in breaking changes in the downstream packages. But the PR is no worse than before so we think we should take it.

As you note, we don't do much validation in the SDK, this is for a few reasons that I know of:

1. The SDK is designed to be a fairly thin wrapper of the API, validation requires additional complexity and more frequent updates to keep aligned with the API
2. If we miss an API change and don't update the SDK accordingly, or users need to be using an older version for compatibility reasons, then they are stuck with incorrect or inconsistent SDK side validation.
3. The SDK is used in release-testing-tools to test API validation of bad inputs

That said, the one place I could maybe get behind more aggressive SDK side validation would be in helper classes like `FlowCreateRequest` as it is possible to work around points 2 and 3 by passing a custom dict to the client method. I think I would prefer leaving the SDK a fairly thin wrapper even in these classes though.

Something to note:

When a process forks we set `TelemetryWriter._forked = True` and prevent [appstarted](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L203), [appclosed](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L228) and [heartbeat](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L215) events from being queued ([link](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L57)). These events should only be sent on the "application" level not on individual processes.

Hey @harshithakolipaka, welcome and great to see you improving our documentation! I've left a few suggestions. Please feel encouraged to ask if something is not clear to you. :slightly_smiling_face: 

I am a bit surprised that you chose to add docstrings to our test suite. Files and functions that start with `test_` are usually part of our test suite and not of our official API. It's not a bad thing to add them here, however, it's also not strictly necessary. :wink: 

However, I think it would be useful to add these docstrings to our ["Adapting gray-scale filters to RGB images" tutorial](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/a4558e13c1b6db7d88d55f4a71fd97702a079427/doc/examples/color_exposure/plot_adapt_rgb.py). That tutorial uses `adapt_rgb` in a similar manner. 

You are referencing #6761. Perhaps the description of that issue is a bit confusing. The goal of #6761 is not to add docstrings to every function anywhere, but to add it to each submodule. In Python that means, adding a docstring at the beginning of each `__init__.py` file. You can see an example of this here https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/a4558e13c1b6db7d88d55f4a71fd97702a079427/skimage/io/__init__.py#L1-L5

```python

from radis import SpectrumFactory
from radis.test.utils import getTestFile
from radis.tools.database import load_spec

my_file = getTestFile("CO2_measured_spectrum_4-5um.spec")  # for the example here

s_exp.crop(4120, 4790).plot(Iunit="mW/cm2/sr/nm")


sf = SpectrumFactory(
    2100,
    2400,  # cm-1
    molecule="CO2",
    isotope="1,2,3",
    wstep=0.002,
)

sf.fetch_databank("hitemp")

s = sf.eq_spectrum_gpu_explore(
    var="radiance",
    Tgas=ParamRange(300.0, 2500.0, 1100.0),  # K
    pressure=ParamRange(0.1, 2, 1),  # bar
    mole_fraction=ParamRange(0, 1, 0.8),
    path_length=ParamRange(0, 1, 0.2),  # cm
    slit_FWHM=ParamRange(0, 1.5, 0.24),  # cm-1
    emulate=True,  # runs on CPU
    plotkwargs={"nfig": "same", "wunit": "nm"},
)

Unfortunately compatibility with `typing` is a bit cursed: `from __future__ import annotations` is in [limbo](https://docs.python.org/3.11/library/__future__.html#id2) (note the lack of resolution for 3.12). If that ever gets removed, we need to make sure we use that version's types at runtime -- of course, it shouldn't be. It's likely that we'll have to change it to [`co_annotations`](https://peps.python.org/pep-0649/) or similar soon. We'll just have to take this as we can. A good next step would be adding `mypy` to `ci.sh` to run it on every platform we test (other than PyPy ones, of course).
